attrs.alt: External ID User Flow event sequence

content.text: User flows in Microsoft Entra External ID define the end-to-end authentication and registration experience for your customers. A user flow specifies which identity providers are offered, what attributes are collected during sign-up, how the UI is branded, and which API connectors run at each step. Each flow is bound to one or more apps.

User flows use an event-driven model internally called authenticationEventsFlows. Each step in the flow — start, IdP selection, attribute collection, account creation — fires a corresponding event that you can intercept with an API connector.
Whether sign-up is allowed for this flow
Validate, enrich, block, or override attributes
User type (member vs guest) to create
Query the authenticationEventsFlows collection to list all user flows and their configuration. Each flow has a priority that determines which flow applies when a sign-in matches multiple flows.

A complete user flow configuration specifies the conditions (which apps it applies to), the available identity providers, the attributes to collect, and the user type to create. Bind the flow to specific app registrations via the conditions.applications array.

API connectors plug custom logic into specific events in the user flow. The connector is any HTTPS endpoint that accepts a JSON request and returns one of the supported action responses. Azure Functions are a natural fit — they deploy alongside your app infrastructure and share the same authentication model.
Linking an API Connector to a Flow
After registering the API connector endpoint, link it to the specific event in the user flow using a PATCH on the flow resource.

Set company branding at the tenant level — it applies to all user flows. For per-locale customization, create branding localization objects. The sign-in page text, logo, background, and button colors are all configurable.
Field is required — sign-up blocked if not provided
Field is shown but customer can skip it
Field value must be verified (e.g., email OTP)
Field is not shown in the form but may be set by API connector
Monitoring User Flow Activity with KQL
Track sign-up funnel conversion rates using Audit Logs. Correlate flow start events with attribute collection completions and user creation to identify drop-off points in the registration experience.

// Track sign-up completions and drop-offs by user flow
// Events correlation: onInteractiveAuthFlowStart → onAttributeCollectionSubmit → onUserCreateStart
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where OperationName in (
    'User flow activity - sign up started',
    'User flow activity - attributes collected',
    'Create user'
)
| extend FlowName = tostring(AdditionalDetails[0].value)
| summarize
    SignUpStarted    = countif(OperationName == 'User flow activity - sign up started'),
    AttrCollected    = countif(OperationName == 'User flow activity - attributes collected'),
    UsersCreated     = countif(OperationName == 'Create user')
    by FlowName, bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
| extend DropOffRate = round(100.0 * (SignUpStarted - UsersCreated) / SignUpStarted, 1)
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Collect minimal attrs at sign-up, more later in-app
Binding a Flow to Multiple Apps

A single user flow can serve multiple app registrations. This is useful when a web app and its companion mobile app share the same sign-up experience. Update the conditions.applications array to include all app client IDs.

The native authentication API lets mobile and desktop apps embed the sign-up/sign-in UI directly without browser redirects. The app calls MSAL's initiate_sign_in_flow and handles OTP collection inline — no system browser or webview required.
Native auth requires 'Enable native authentication' to be toggled on in the user flow settings in the External ID portal before it responds to native auth requests.

# Native authentication API: embed sign-up form directly in iOS/Android/desktop app
# No browser redirect needed — MSAL calls the /initiateSignIn endpoint directly
# Requires: enable native authentication in the user flow settings

# Python MSAL example using native auth (public preview)
import msal

CLIENT_ID = 'your-app-client-id'
TENANT_ID = 'your-ciam-tenant.onmicrosoft.com'
AUTHORITY = f'https://your-ciam-tenant.ciamlogin.com/{TENANT_ID}'

app = msal.PublicClientApplication(CLIENT_ID, authority=AUTHORITY)

# Step 1: initiate sign-in with email
result = app.initiate_sign_in_flow(
    scopes=['openid', 'profile'],
    login_hint='customer@example.com'
)

# Step 2: if OTP sent, collect from user input and submit
if result.get('flow_token'):
    otp = input('Enter the one-time code sent to your email: ')
    token = app.acquire_token_by_otp(result['flow_token'], otp)
    if 'access_token' in token:
        print('Signed in successfully')

Create a separate user flow for profile editing. Set isSignUpAllowed to false and include only the attributes you want customers to be able to update. Trigger this flow from your app's profile settings page using an OIDC prompt=login redirect.
Deleting a user flow breaks sign-in for all apps bound to it. Always check which apps are bound before deleting, and either migrate them to another flow first or delete the apps simultaneously.

# Delete a user flow (caution: breaks sign-in for any apps bound to it)
$flowId = "aaaabbbb-flow-id-1234"

# Check which apps are bound to this flow before deleting
$flow = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/authenticationEventsFlows/$flowId"
$apps = $flow.conditions.applications.includeApplications
Write-Warning "This flow is bound to $($apps.Count) app(s): $($apps.appId -join ', ')"

# Prompt for confirmation before proceeding
$confirm = Read-Host "Type DELETE to confirm removal of flow $($flow.displayName)"
if ($confirm -eq 'DELETE') {
    Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method DELETE \
        -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/authenticationEventsFlows/$flowId"
    Write-Host 'User flow deleted'
} else {
    Write-Host 'Deletion cancelled'
}

The AzureAD Terraform provider does not yet natively expose authenticationEventsFlows. Until native support lands, manage user flows via the Azure portal or use a null_resource with az rest calls. Store the flow JSON configuration in version control alongside the Terraform code.
User flows define the customer authentication and registration experience: IdP selection, attribute collection, branding, and token output.
Each flow is an event sequence; API connectors intercept events to validate, enrich, or block sign-ups at each stage.
Attribute collection configuration controls which profile fields are shown, which are required, and which need verification.
Company branding and per-locale customization let you match the sign-in page to your product's visual identity.
Flow priority determines which flow applies when a customer signs in to an app that matches multiple flows.
Monitor sign-up funnel conversion via Audit Logs using KQL to identify where customers abandon registration.
